01---Projects/psm/AI-Advertise-1.title: AI Advertise 1
01---Projects/psm/AI-Advertise-1.content:
Hello! That’s a great project to work on for learning Rust and cryptography! Let’s discuss your file-based storage design and potential improvements.
💾 File vs. Database Storage
Regarding your question about using individual files (folder + files) versus a database:
The approach of using AES-encrypted files for your data is a good and common approach for a minimal, local password manager (like the Unix pass or KeePass).
✅ Advantages of Encrypted Files:
Simplicity and Minimal Dependencies: You avoid adding a database engine (like SQLite) dependency, making your project lighter and easier to manage. This aligns well with your goal of making it minimal.

Security (with proper encryption): Since all the files are encrypted with AES, they are just as secure as an encrypted database file—provided your master key derivation (using a KDF like Argon2) is strong and your AES implementation (e.g., using AES-256-GCM) is sound.

Easy Backup/Sync: The user only needs to back up/sync the entire directory of encrypted files.

⚠️ Disadvantage of Encrypted Files:
Metadata Leakage: This is the biggest security concern with a file-per-entry system. Even though the contents of your password files are encrypted, the filenames, directory names, file sizes, and timestamps are often visible to an attacker who compromises the file system but not your encryption key.

    Example: If you name your files after the service (e.g., github.pass, bank.pass), an attacker instantly knows the list of all services you have accounts for.

Performance: For a large number of entries, iterating over hundreds or thousands of files in a directory can be slower than querying a single database file.

💡 Recommendation: Use a Single Encrypted Vault File
To maintain simplicity but solve the metadata leakage problem, the most popular approach for local managers is to use a single, encrypted file (a “vault”) that contains all your password entries and metadata (like the KeePass .kdbx format).
This way, an attacker only sees one file with a generic name (e.g., vault.bin or psm.dat), and all metadata (service names, usernames, etc.) are hidden within the file’s encrypted contents.
🚀 Suggested Project Improvements
Here are some improvements and best practices to consider for your Rust password manager:


🛡️ Cryptographic Best Practices
Key Derivation Function (KDF): Ensure you are using a modern, memory-hard, and time-costly KDF like Argon2 (specifically Argon2id) to derive the strong encryption key from the user’s master password. Hashing the master password directly (like with SHA-256) is not secure enough.
 Check: Are you using a KDF with an appropriate salt, iterations/time cost, memory cost, and parallelism settings?

Authenticated Encryption: Ensure you are using an authenticated encryption mode for AES, like AES-256-GCM. This not only encrypts the data but also provides an integrity check (a MAC) to ensure the encrypted data hasn’t been tampered with. It looks like you’re using aes_gcm, which is great!


📝 Secret Handling in Memory
Memory Zeroing: Sensitive data, like the master password or decrypted passwords, should be zeroed out (overwritten with zeroes) from memory as soon as they are no longer needed. This prevents them from being unintentionally leaked by memory inspection or saved to disk in hibernation/swap files.
 Rust Crates: Look into crates like zeroize or secrecy that provide wrapper types (like Secret<T>) to handle memory zeroing automatically upon drop.

Command Line Argument Security: Never accept the master password as a direct command-line argument (e.g., psm login —password “my_pass”). Command-line arguments can be read by other users on the system (via ps aux or Task Manager) and are often saved in shell history files.
 Solution: Use interactive prompt for the master password (e.g., using a crate like rpassword to read the input securely without echoing) or read it from standard input (stdin).



✨ Usability and Features
Password Generation: A password manager is incomplete without a built-in strong, random password generator. This is a high-value feature for security and convenience.
Clipboard Management: Implement logic to copy the decrypted password to the clipboard and then automatically clear the clipboard after a short timeout (e.g., 30-60 seconds). This mitigates the risk of the password being left in the clipboard buffer.


I hope this helps you move forward with your awesome project!
Would you like me to look into a specific section of your code (e.g., how you handle the master password or the encryption) to provide more targeted feedback?
By the way, to unlock the full functionality of all Apps, enable Gemini Apps Activity.

use a ‘vault’ like file instead of saving all password inside a folder, because we could have metadata leaking system.
use interactive command line approach instead of per-line things, because the shell often save the commands and this can be bad.

02---Blogs/web/Let's-have-a-talk....title: Let's have a talk...
02---Blogs/web/Let's-have-a-talk....content:
Hello and Hello after a while! I’m here again and writing blogs to talk about my inner thoughts Xd.
Well let’s be honest everything changed and I think I’m growing like a real person (at least I hope so)
I made a blog post about my tend to make games, well… it didn’t go well in some senses. First of all, I really like the game development and I believe I need more learning curves for it xd. But the thing is… right now I can’t make any effort from it. I really like the programming side of it and this is not actually enough for someone who is not great at art, I know learning & trying can make the best of the best, but it’s not for me right now. But the thing is… I still make games in my free times, and I still love the process as I always do.
But you may ask, Ok eiko… but what do you do now? well it’s a great question, I returned to back-end development xd, Yea you hear it right, I’m going to start the process from zero (I did started btw) and try to fill the gaps that I had and call myself a back-end developer. It’s patient and some logical reasons in the same time. patient because I like it obviously and logical reasons are things like financial situation and some other things. Yes that gd financial situation!. I don’t know you noticed this before, making games is not great for someone who needs money (Specially if you didn’t used to it) and wants to actually do something that has financial future. It’s great that some games sell for millions and dollars but the thing is… it’s not a promise job, and you may not gain anything at all!
But again, I will do it as hobby and maybe someday I made something cool and exciting :).
Oh well, let’s more talk about the concepts instead of my situation. I started back-end developing as a hobby because I wanted to make my website more interactive to others. I did some PHP and I switched to Flask, A simple minimal (Maybe it’s feature rich right now, idk) python library to work with web servers. It was find for me and I liked to do anything by my own. That was not a problem for that old version of me, because I was literally 14 or 15 years old, and imagine how much you could do without the how much fun you could have with a minimal architecture library that lets you do anything by your own. but that same reason, made me a very strict person in the sense of learning things. I used to be a programming that wants to do anything without using libraries and ready-to-go frameworks. that’s why I have never learned Django, Another python framework for back-end stuff. after some years (now i’m 21) I can feel this pressure that the right option is to learn by standard and try to understand that there is some code that is more well structures than mind and sometime I need to calm down and let others do magical stuff for me. yea… it has been very hard for me to change, but I’m trying. All of the things that i said was the bridge to learn more love level stuff and trying to do anything by my own. that’s why I started learning C/C++ after python and step inside the real stuff. I learned C, Make, C++, low level compiler concepts like compiler, linking, dynamic libraries, etc. and I forgot that I need to continue learn the thing that I started, back-end development
I always liked the concept of back-end development. It feels like you are writing real software (i’m sorry front-end guys, I’m not just not good at speaking Xd) and you can so cool stuff. Same stuff that you do on your system-base programs and just turn them into a web application. Literally just work with I/O data that comes and goes as HTTP requests. you have an interface that works with HTTP (or maybe you are supporting more network protocols) and you have your main code that works by anything (F.g creating online game logic). that is a great thing cause now you write software and you connect it to some sort of network stack that can open new gates of ideas for you. That’s why I love back-end development and it feels good to do it.
I did some projects related to web development and learned one of the biggest things in my life, Golang. It’s very funny cause I learned it just because I wanted to learn something new and volla, it just made me a different person. But you may guess why I like it since I explained that I’m a person who likes minimalism. after learning golang I decided to do more projects (I’m talking about 1 year ago) and I did some. But the thing is I still didn’t learned it the way I should. I just learn interested things of it and made some projects, but this is not enough for someone to be a real Software engineer. Now, in these days I decided to start over and grow from basic stuff to professional concepts and fill that gap for myself. For sure I can learn more quickly as I have some background in this profession. I have been struggling with some thoughts like maybe i’m late. But the reality is you start from a point and get better at that point, it doesn’t matter when you start, it matter how you keep going and how much patient you are. If you don’t start now you will say: I wish I started before and this is though alone can make more nested thoughts like maybe i’m late. No, you are not late. you just need to start right now and prevent yourself saying that thing again in the future. Well i can say it is working for me now. I feel better and I feel like I a goal in my life.
Well Hope you’ll have a great time, and like always, Happy Coding

02---Blogs/web/Why-IndieWeb.title: Why IndieWeb?
02---Blogs/web/Why-IndieWeb.content:
Greetings, have you ever ask yourself what is the purpose of creating handmade websites as a non-technical person using coding language likes HTML/CSS or even Javascript? well if it is then seems like you know what indie web is, but if you don’t know what exactly is that term, well I’ll got you.
We use Indie web as a term for people who make personal (sometimes even non-personal) websites with their own creativity and host those websites by themselves. but for sure there is multiple free hosting platforms that do this automaticly like Neocities, Nekoweb, etc.
This

02---Blogs/web/my-new-noting-website.title: my new noting website
02---Blogs/web/my-new-noting-website.content:
Well hello there! and greetings to this corner of the indie web. Today I want to showcase one of the things that I learned (still learning) and maybe it will inspire you for your next works. As always I was taking notes of my works and writing some day diary for myself that I got an idea, what if I could turn my development website that was nothing but just a direct link to my Github page into my technical notes, then all the technical and even non-technical users can read it and I can make a closer communication to my users.
As someone who doesn’t have much friends and social interactions, I have to find a way to interact with people and one way that I found appealing is writing blogs and maintain my personal website. As I progress in this beautiful world I found multiple friends and other people who have the same taste as me and it’s really feels like 20s :> but one thing that always was my question is that how can i make a very reliable structure to format for my personal website, blog, the favorite things that I like and in the same time make them visually look good. I got the idea to make a static site generation tool but as you may know there is multiple great options available, but as you know may not know I’m a very From Scratch person, then I made my own tool called Staticy that was not a fully static site generation tool (it supposed to be that but I didn’t continue it) but it was a blogging tool, it can turn your markdown files into static html files the dynamic template stuff. I have multiple idea for it but after all that idea was not unique and finally it’s just my own toy project that I use to generate my blogs (yes even this blog xd). Anyway, I said those things to get to the point that I realized a personal website can be literally anything, and because 90% of the times I do nothing but writing, it’s a great fit for me. And one more thing that was really appealing for me was the fact that people don’t read/write anymore, and this website can be a great point to inspire other people to write/read. Well this time again I said I want to make my own program/website. my idea was a website that uses modern technologies (reacting client only pages) that can turn my markdown files into html but with a better structure + an environment for me to experience custom script that make cool things while the UI is still retro and simple/minimal for the old web. in a nut-shell a minimal personal website using modern programming stack that uses the same design of my current website (this web space). Well that was great bmecause i wanted to learn a new UI framework while I was working on that project, my goal is a framework called ‘Svelte’ that sounds very cool to me.
But later that time I said, what if i turn my obsidian vault into a website, you may ask why? because 90% of the things that i post in my website is just pure text. I’m a writer and i didn’t know about it lol. Well that was a thing, because at first, AGAIN i ranted to make my own tool but after a quick search I got a tool that already made for this job. A tool called Quartz. It’s interesting but a bit complicated for just a tool that converts obsidian vault into html files (it’s an static site generation with the focus of obsidian environment structure). why it’s a bit complicated? because it has its own internal Component system :>.
Well anyway, I installed it and using it was a charm, It’s easy to build/update your obsidian notes with quartz, plus it has a dynamic system that you can literally change a lot of things if you don’t like it (it’s like a double edge sword, it’s dynamic but not minimal).
Well here we are. I still want to re-make my website with Svelte but probably i’ll write my primary development contents inside my Fractuna web space.
Oh btw, My goal is to make a community inside the Youtube and post technical/programming contents inside it, that way of communication is the only way that I can express myself. For sure I’ll write another blog post for that topic and introduce you to the concept of communication through writing.
Until that time, have a great nerding time!

03---Study/Machines-Automata/Concepts/Wh.filePath: 03 - Study/Machines Automata/Concepts/What is Machine Automata theory.md
03---Study/Machines-Automata/Concepts/Wh.title: What is Machine Automata theory
03---Study/Machines-Automata/Concepts/Wh.content:
Automata theory is the study of abstract machines and Automata, and how or what tasks can we solve with them.
A machine operating with finite number of states is called Finite Automaton (FA).
OR Finite State Machine (FSM). 
This concept brings a new question, What if we want to have a virtual language, what we should consider?.
Symbols
A language has multiple symbols (alphabets), things like: a,b,c,d, etc.
we call these symbols.
Strings
String is composed of multiple arbitrary Symbols.
Language
Language is composed of multiple Symbols with specific and regular structure.
but a accurate and exact explanation is:

A collection of strings that consist from a collection of alphabets is language.

which means:

\Sigma > language (language is under the sigma of a sigma star)

we usually show the language alias name with letter ‘L’.
Language Operator
Reverse String (^R)
We represent the Reverse of a string using R sign.
Example:

w=abb -> w^R=bba

Concatenation String (.)
We represent string concatenation with ’.’ (dot)
Example:

w1=aa, w2=bb -> w1.w2=aabb

Power String (^X)
We represent the power of a string (alias Concatenation String (.)) with math power operation symbol.
Examples:

w^2=w.w
w^3=w.w.w=w^2.w
w=ab -> w=abab

Empty/Zero String (\lambda)
We represent/call strings that has size equal to zero (empty) Lambda (\lambda) (Half life reference) or fsnull* or Epsilon.
Closure of String (^*)
There is infinity amount of powers that we can give to Strings, examples are:

w^0, w^1, w^2, w^3 … w^X

But there is a simple symbol that represents all those values and states, we can show collection of All possible powers from zero to infinity by using ^*.
Example:

w^*

we call this representation Closure or Kleene closure.
There is 2 types of a Kleene closure:

\Sigma^* → from 0 to infinity, means 0 or more repetitions
\Sigma^+ → from 1 to infinity, means 1 or more repetitions

Notice: we can show all the possible Words without using any variables like this:

\Sigma^* = collection of all strings on \Sigma including λ

Language Representation
it’s a way to represent the Language.
Regular Expression
This is a good way of representing Language
Example:

show words that start with A and ends with B

Grammer
Grammer basically do the same thing but it is a way more professional way of representing a language than Regular Expression.
Example:
S = 
\begin{cases}
S \to AB \\
A \to a \\
B \to b
\end{cases}
Variables: Are shows with uppercase letters
Values: Are shows with lowercase letter
Automata
Automata or self acting is the a self-operating machine with the goal of running sequence instructions or response.
based on wiki:

An automaton (automata in plural) is an abstract self-propelled computing device which follows a predetermined sequence of operations automatically.

Refs:

Online Session
en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automaton

03---Study/Software-Engineering/Concepts.filePath: 03 - Study/Software Engineering/Concepts/1.0 - What is Software Engineering.md
03---Study/Software-Engineering/Concepts.title: 1.0 - What is Software Engineering
03---Study/Software-Engineering/Concepts.content:
First, we need to understand what does Software means.
What is software?
Software consists of 3 different components (CAN 1):

Software Code
Software Data
Software Document

With that said, we can make a conclusion that:
Software Engineering is an approach and structure for creating good and reliable software.
this means using any of those components alone will not produce Software And only the combination of those components produce the Software.
Software Code
The main code that shapes the behavior of the software.
Software Data
This component can include any shape of data like:

Data structure
Databases
etc

Software Document
Document is simply the explanation/Document of the code.

A code without the documentation is not a Software, it is just a piece of code

This component can include:

developer documentations
end-user documentation

Software Domains

System Software: A Software/program that makes an reliable interface for higher-level programs (F.g Drivers, Operating Systems, etc.)
Application Software: High-level application for end-users that uses low-level System Software to interact with the system (F.g terminals, Media players, Browsers)
Engineering and Scientific: For engineers or scientists to Design scientific stuff. (F.g Matlab)
Embedded Software: Software that runs on specific, low resource hardware like Micro Controllers. (F.g Embedded AI Models)
Product-Line Software: Software with the goal of internal customization for each and individual customers that mostly has finite functions and high usage. (F.g Wordpress)
Web/Mobile Software: Application software that runs on top of mobile operating system like Android, IOS OR browser engines like Firefox. Notice there is also some hybrid software that uses both at the same time. (F.g Youtube)
AI Software: Software that runs with the goal of artificial intelligence.

Refs:

youtu.be/BRDTco_ySnM (Part 1)
youtu.be/9nvNj5opAVQ (Part 2)

03---Study/Software-Engineering/Concepts.title: 1.1 - Legacy Software
03---Study/Software-Engineering/Concepts.content:
Legacy
A set of software that can be in active mode and outdated in the same time, A good examples can be:

ATM machines that still runs DOS or any old operating system.
Active factories that still use Fortran programming language
Embedded devices with Assembly codes (not uses high level C compilers)

This means regardless of begin outdated, the software is still workable.
Legacy Software
There is this concept of Legacy Software in the software engineering world.
The first question is why we should keep using them? the answer is:

When a software works, there is no need to replace it. Replacing/re-writing a software is hard.

Most of the time, the hardware is the problem, when we see a hardware problem we have to change the corresponding software too, that’s why usually stable software keep stable until hardware is going to become absolute.
but legacy software has it’s own hard times, including:

High/Expensive maintenance cost
Rare technical knowledge

But still there is ways to fix those problems such as:

Emulation software (E.g QEMU)

Refs:

youtu.be/z6pWJ6o1OzU (Part 3)

03---Study/Software-Engineering/Concepts.title: 1.2 Visualization Technologies
03---Study/Software-Engineering/Concepts.content:
Virtualization
A way to run multiple operating system in one Physical hardware. (CAN 1.2 1.2)
As we are using just 1 hardware, then all the resources will be shared among N instances of visualized operating systems.
Example:
A machine with 32GB of RAM and 4 cores could visualized as below:

























Machine OSRAMCORELinux Debian82Linux Ubuntu81Unix81
Those VMs will have their own software stack, means their own kernel and other programs.
Visualization Technologies
In the old days of technology, big companies that wanted to have a dynamic online service (like shopping web pages) tend to buy multiple servers for each technical component of their technical stack (F.g LAMP), Because:

Vulnerability
Reliability
Extendability
more

and that would cost them a lot, just imagine buying multiple Physical servers to run 1 web application. In late 2000s technical developers made a solution to solve that problem called Virtualization. Also it’s worth mentioning this technology was not new (came from late 1960s).
Then we can say the benefits of Virtualization is:

Low Cost
More Security
Better Management
Easier Maintenance

Popular Virtualization companies and programs are:

VMWare

Esxi
Vsphere


Xen

KVM (merged to Linux itself)


Hyver-V
VirtualBox
QEMU
Parallels

Refs:

youtu.be/v4uPrpwcnuw (Part 4)

04---Diary/Russian-Khrushchevka-Houses.title: Russian Khrushchevka Houses
04---Diary/Russian-Khrushchevka-Houses.content:
Stalinka
Between 1930 and 1950 with the leader ship of Staling, Russian had beautiful, complex and well made apartment design that was rich in the term of design level
It’s important to mention that buildings like this constructed in industrial are of Russian.
Khrushchevka
Russian Khrushchevka houses came from the old days of world war 2.
Before khrushchevka style houses, we had stalinka houses. but after world war 2 Russians (specifically Nikita Khrushchev) started to make houses that was easy to build, simple (somehow minimal?), very compact and affordable for all Russians citizens.
It’s very important that Russian made this type houses as temporary houses, and that’s why they were very simple and chip. It was a promise for future comfortable houses by rising the Soviet Union and Communism.
the building of this type of apartment continued until 1960’s.
Padyezd (подъезд)
A term in Russian about the ‘entrance of the house’ (or lobby)
Brezhnevkas
A improved type of building after the area of Khrushchevka apartments. this type of apartments were bigger with more space and elevator built-in on them.
the buildings of this type of apartments continued until 1990’s.
Refs:
- My Russian Apartment Tour: GREY SOVIET BUILDINGS EXPLAINED!

04---Diary/Unix-shared-system.title: Unix shared system
04---Diary/Unix-shared-system.content:
Hello, I was searching about the indie web that I got something very cool! there is a concept called Tilde that users use a share Unix base operating system for creating stuff such as web pages.
Recording to the main wiki of Tilde:

A tilde is a multi-user online system designed to be a “home” for users by providing services such as shell access via SSH, email, small site hosting (on www, gopher, gemini), chat, or other programs. It is a loose term, akin to a public access unix systems (a.k.a. pubnixes), but with some connotations of a small community.

Well this idea seems like fun and in the same time a very cool way of hosting pages. The main idea of sharing your operating system is very cool! it’s like a minimal version of what you expect from VPSs to work through network everywhere.

05---Books/Network-programming-with-Go.title: Network programming with Go
05---Books/Network-programming-with-Go.content:
Notes

07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.title: Clean Architecture
07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.content:
Clean Architecture book

There are some principles that we can use to have a clean architecture project:

Separation of concerns: Each level in your program should be separated by clear barrier, the transport layer, the service layer, the storage layer…
Dependencies inversion Principles (DIP): Your’s injecting the dependencies in your layer. You don’t directly call them. Why? it promotes loose coupling and makes it easier to test your program.
Adaptability to Change: By organizing your code in a module and flexible way, you can more easily introduce more features, refactor existing code, and response to evolving business requirements.
Focus on business values: and finally, focus on delivering value to your users, they are the ones who will paying your bills at the end of the month. So focus on the business value.

With all that said, we can separate our software to these layers (SOC):

Transport: the way that we deliver message to our users, like HTTP or Grpc
Service: business logic lives (optional)
Storage: abstracts the communication above and below, which is database or any type of storage


Injection dependencies focus on abstraction not implementation (interface > struct)

for example we can inject a mock service or database to test the program without using any real code bases.
we should make our application configurable and at runtime regardless where it’s deployed.
Things I found interesting

saga pattern: it’s like we make a revert transaction system for a component

07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.title: DNS
07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.content:
DNS (Domain Name System): The Internet’s Phonebook
DNS is a hierarchical, distributed naming system that translates human-readable domain names (like example.com) into numerical IP addresses (like 93.184.216.34) that computers use to identify each other on the network.

Purpose: Eliminates the need for users to remember complex IP addresses.
Hierarchical Structure:

Root Servers: The top of the hierarchy, knows where to find Top-Level Domain (TLD) servers.
TLD Servers: Manage domains like .com, .org, .net, .io. They know which authoritative name servers are responsible for specific domains.
Authoritative Name Servers: Hold the actual DNS records for a domain (e.g., example.com) and are the final authority for those names.


DNS Resolution Process:

User types example.com.
Local DNS resolver (often provided by ISP) receives the query.
Resolver queries a root server, which points to the .com TLD server.
Resolver queries the .com TLD server, which points to example.com’s authoritative name server.
Resolver queries the authoritative name server, which returns the IP address for example.com.
The IP address is returned to the user’s browser, which then connects to that IP.


Caching: DNS queries are heavily cached at various levels (browser, OS, local resolver) to speed up subsequent lookups.
Record Types:

A (Address): Maps a domain name to an IPv4 address.
AAAA (Quad-A): Maps a domain name to an IPv6 address.
CNAME (Canonical Name): Creates an alias from one domain name to another.
MX (Mail Exchange): Specifies mail servers responsible for accepting email for a domain.
NS (Name Server): Specifies the authoritative name servers for a domain.
TXT (Text): Holds arbitrary text, often used for verification or SPF records.
SRV (Service): Specifies hostname and port for specific services.


Importance for Backend:

Service Discovery: Backend microservices often use internal DNS records to find each other.
Load Balancing: DNS Round Robin (distributing traffic by returning different IP addresses for the same domain) is a basic form of load balancing.
CDN Integration: CDNs use DNS to direct users to the nearest edge location.

07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.title: HTTP
07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.content:
Stateless
the server is stateless, it means client for each actions - each time needs to request to the web server
benefits

Simplcity
scalability

because that HTTP is stateless, then developers often imlements state managment technichs such as:

coockies
session
tokens
for things like user logins, or shoping, etc.

It’s very important that client always initial the request.
Before sending anything, client and server needs to make a connection, they will provide it with TCP (Transfer Layer Protocol) that will initial the connection between 2 devices.
HTTP Versions
HTTP 1.0
Each request open a new connection
HTTP 1.1
Enable multiple request and response over the same connectino
Caching
HTTP 2.0
Multiplxing: multiple request or responses over a single connection
header compression
server push: server send resouces before client sends them
HTTP 3.0
Quick Protocol: transport layer over UDP, instead of TCP
Imporoved performance, reduce latency and packet lost
multiplexing without headofline blocking (still an issue in HTTP 2.0)
HTTP Request/Response/Header Structure
Request
PUT /api/users/12345 HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
User-Agnet: Mozzila/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64)
...
 
// a blank line here means, everything has been sent
 
{
	"firstName": "John",
	"lastName": "Wing",
	"email": "wing@gmail.com",
	"age": 30
}
Response
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Fri, 20 Sep 2024 12:00:00 GMT
Content-Type: application/json
....
 
// a blank line here means, everything has been sent
 
{
	"message": "User updated succsessfully",
	"userId": 12345,
	"status": "success"
}
Headers
Headers are key-value pair of different perramiter, but why we use beaders?:
Headers are actual meta data values that we send to server or recieve from server to make a proper connection. Also headers have more use cases.
example of useful header keys/values:
Request headers

User-Agent: identifies what kind of client is that
Authorization: to identifies the user (use cases like tokens)
Accept: which provide information what kind of content we are expect

General headers
we use this type of header both in req and resp

Date: date of the message
Cache-Control: Caching mechanism
Connection: connection information like keep it alive or close it.

Representation headers
Usually specifies the way to represent the data

Content-Type: the actual type of content (json, image, HTML)
Content-Length: The length of the whole size of the resouce packet in bytes
Content-Encoding: which kind of encoding does data has (like gzip, diffler)
ETag: unique Identify which mostly uses per cache

Security Headers
Enhance the security of request/response by controlling the behavior of caching and, etc.

Strick-Transport-Security (HSTS): Insuring that client only communicate with server, or https preventing protocol downgrade.
Content-Security-Policy (CSP): strict which content like js,image,html can be loaded
X-Frame-Options: Prevents the page to be embedded in iframe
X-Content-Type-Options: Prevent the browser to quess the mimetype
Set-Cookie: Secure cookies that are accesiable to javascript, and ensuring they are sending over the HTTPS

Headers can be used in more ares because of:

Extensibility: when we use headers, we can use/add features without modifies the underlying HTTP/HTTP protocol, some of them can even be custom that made by other devs (X-* headers)
Remove Control: We can use header to actually control the behavior of server by providing some options inside the header, for example we can authenticate the user or server can set a cache mechanism to cache the data for client browser, or we can set our own custom header for more extensible functions.

HTTP Methods
Http method are exists to represent different kinds of actions by client, like browser or api consumer. For example If we want to get the name of the user, we can set the Intent of our request, then we can functions differently than for example modify it.
Http methods are:

GET: we use it to get some resources from the server
POST: We use it to put some resources to the server, it has the body to set data from the client
PUT: Update some resources from the server but it should replace whatever we provide as body to the target resource (Complete Replacement)
PATCH: Update some resources from the server but it should only patch (modify) the part of the resource that we provided as body (Modify only needed parts)
DELETE: We use to delete a resource from the server.
OPTIONS (CORS): it used to fetch the capability of the server for Cross Origin Requests (CORS), it controls how browser should communicate the the server resources from different host addresses.

Note that Host is used for the target api that we want to request.
And we use Origin is used for our own local domain (or maybe a domain from a server).
Idempotent or non-idempotent
Please See The Idempotency section.
Response Code
they will return the final state or situation of functioned request from the server as a code, this code perfectly specifies what was the exact result of the request.
This code is standard and works across all platform, languages, systems, etc.
On High level response codes composed from:

Information:  1xx
Success:      2xx
Redirection:  3xx
Client Error: 4xx
Server Error: 5xx

for example 200 is OK message that means request was succsessful and completed properly by the server.
Or 201 means resource has been fulfill in the reason of Creation a resource (F.g POST Method)
or 204 means request has been fulfill in the reason of accepting content, but there is nothing to return by the server itself (Nothing to return).
301 means move permanently, that means the requested resource has been moved to a new URL, and request should be in that new URL. (F.g backwards compatibility)
302 means move temporarly, that means the request resource has been temporarely moves to a new URL, but the future request will continue uses the main URL. (F.g for testing things or running temp events like campains)
304 means the resource has not been modified since the last request (F.g GET Method, Caching)
400 means bad request, we use to when the data from client is not correct and server needs a better format of data.
401 means Unauthorized, we use this to say you are not authorized or failed at that level (F.g server is using JWT, but client didn’t provide that or it is expired)
403 means Forbidden, we use this when the client tried to access a resource but they don’t have the permission for that (like when we are filtered from other sites xd)
404 means Resource not Found, we use this when client wants some resources that are not available in the server.
405 means Method is not allowed, we use this to give information to client that this method is not allowed for this server or for example for this route.
409 means conflict, we use this for miltiple reason, but for example we can return this code when use wants to add an resource that is already available to the server, and server can say that there is some conflict with available resource here.
429 means too many request, we use this to allow limited amount of requests and if they are succeeds out of that, we return this code (F.g Rate limit systems)
500 means internal server error, we use this to give information about some internal server errors, this is not related to clients and it’s only for internal components of the server. (F.g Something unexpected happend)
501 means not implemented, we use this when the server doesn’t support the HTTP method or any functionality, but it plants to implement it soon.
502 means bad gateway, Usually proxy server use this, they use it to say upstream proxy server returns an invalid response.
503 means service is not available
504 means gateway timeout, for example when we request to a server that uses reverse proxy in front and that reverse proxy cannot access the internal local server (Main web server), after some time (timeout) that reverse proxy can return this code.
Caching
We use this mechanism to recognize the re-use of the resource and cache them then, we can more quckily access the resource.
we usually control cache with HTTP headers, such as:
Cache-Control header
means: you should maintain the cache for this resource for N seconds
Cache-Control: max-age=30, public

In this example, client should maintain the cache for 30 seconds.
ETag header
they are usually hashes of the response message
Etag: 3141

Last-Modified Header
with this header method, we can see what was the last time that resource modifies
Last-Modified: Fri, 28 Sep 2024 13:48:33 GMT

what Usually happens here is that server will send those 3 headers that we talked about to the server, then the next time client request the resources, client will provide those header values with a new key names such as:

If-None-Mache (same as Etag)
If-Modified-Since (Same as Last-Modified)

To the server, and then server will check those with its own values of those headers, if they are same then return the 403 code (Not Modified) and if they are not same, then return the newly created resource to the client. it’s also noticeable that server will add another header called Cache-Control that we talked about, that means please do the new request after this N seconds, until that cache the current data.
Benefits

load time
reduces bandwidth
gain speed

Refrences:

www.youtube.com/watch

07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.title: Idempotency
07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.content:
Idempotency
An operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times produces the same result as applying it once. For network requests, especially in distributed systems, idempotency is crucial because requests can be retried due to network glitches or timeouts.

Example: A POST request to create a new resource is typically not idempotent (sending it twice might create two resources). A PUT request to update a resource is idempotent (sending it multiple times has the same final state).
Backend Best Practice: Design your API endpoints to be idempotent where possible, especially for operations that modify data. If not inherently idempotent, implement mechanisms (e.g., unique transaction IDs) to ensure repeated requests don’t cause unintended side effects.

07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.title: Load Balancers
07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.content:
Load Balancers: Distributing the Workload
Load balancers distribute incoming network traffic across a group of backend servers (a server farm or pool). This ensures high availability, scalability, and improved performance.

Why Needed:

Scalability: Allows adding more backend servers to handle increased traffic.
High Availability: If one server fails, traffic is redirected to healthy servers.
Performance: Prevents a single server from becoming a bottleneck.


Types:

Layer 4 Load Balancers (TCP/UDP): Operate at the transport layer, forwarding traffic based on IP address and port number. Fast and efficient.
Layer 7 Load Balancers (HTTP/HTTPS): Operate at the application layer, understanding HTTP/HTTPS protocols. Can make more intelligent routing decisions based on URL paths, HTTP headers, cookies, etc. Often perform SSL/TLS termination.


Algorithms:

Round Robin: Distributes requests sequentially to each server.
Least Connections: Sends requests to the server with the fewest active connections.
IP Hash: Directs client requests to the same server based on a hash of the client’s IP address (useful for session stickiness without cookies).


Health Checks: Load balancers continuously monitor the health of backend servers and automatically remove unhealthy ones from the pool, redirecting traffic to healthy servers.
Backend Impact: When designing backend services, assume they will be behind a load balancer. Services should be stateless or handle session persistence externally.

07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.title: Repository Pattern

07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.filePath: 07 - Tech/00 - Back-end Programming/Concepts/Repository pattern vs Data Access Layer.md
07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.title: Repository pattern vs Data Access Layer
07---Tech/00---Back-end-Programming/Conc.content:
As we process to focus on domains (F.g User, Game, Shows) in RP and make codes based on that, we try to make a very general and dynamic system for DAL (data access layer). This means that in DAL, we only make the abstraction layer itself and doesn’t work with any meaning or entities, just pure functionality of that subject (mostly database). A great example is GORM, this lib doesn’t have any relation with the types and tables that we are going to make, but it gives us the functionality to work with them (implementations not just domains and subjects).
But in RP, we care about the domain specefic implementation (F.g GetUser, GetEmailByName, GetScore). and this can shows us a very important quote:

Data Access Layer is working underneth the Repository Pattern, what I mean by that is we use DAL inside the RP. We make all functionalities of database and then we use it domain based on RP functions (getUset).

 
type UserRepository struct {
    db *gorm.DB
}
 
func (r *UserRepository) GetByEmail(email string) (*User, error) {
    var u User
    err := r.db.Where("email = ?", email).First(&u).Error
    return &u, err
}
 
func (r *UserRepository) Save(u *User) error {
    return r.db.Save(u).Error
}
 
AI Good Quotes
Repository Pattern is a higher-level abstraction


It works with domain concepts (User, Order, Product)


It hides the DAL from business logic


It contains domain-specific queries


It returns domain models instead of rows


It is specific: one repository per aggregate/entity


Think of Repository as: “How do I retrieve or persist domain objects?”
How they work together (perfect combo)
🔽 Low-level
DAL / GORM / SQLX / pgx / raw SQL
Handles database logic.
🔼 High-level
Repository Layer
Uses DAL to fetch/update domain models.
🔼 Higher-level
Service Layer (business logic)
Uses repositories to execute business rules.
🔼 Top-level
Handlers / Controllers (HTTP)
Call services.

07---Tech/02---Game-Programming/Godot/Go.title: Godot 3D Doc notes
07---Tech/02---Game-Programming/Godot/Go.content:
General

To find a vector pointing from A to B, use B - A.

Scalar

Vectors represent both direction and magnitude (length). A value representing only magnitude is called a scalar. Scalars use the float type in Godot.

Unit vectors
A vector with magnitude of 1 is called a unit vector. They are also sometimes referred to as direction vectors or normals. Unit vectors are helpful when you need to keep track of a direction.
Normalization
Normalizing a vector means reducing its length to 1 while preserving its direction. This is done by dividing each of its components by its magnitude. Because this is such a common operation, Godot provides a dedicated normalized() method for this:
Q&As
Read more about Dot Product and Cross Product.
Gilbert Strang lectures on Linear Algebra (MIT) for better understanding of linear algebra.

07---Tech/04---Go/Concepts/Refrences.title: Refrences
07---Tech/04---Go/Concepts/Refrences.content:
In Go, a weak pointer refers to a reference that does not prevent the garbage collector (GC) from reclaiming the target object.
When an object is only referenced by weak pointers and has no strong references, the GC will still treat it as unreachable and reclaim it; afterwards, all weak pointers to it will automatically become nil.
In short, a weak pointer does not increase an object’s reference count. When an object is referenced only by weak pointers, the garbage collector can free it. Therefore, before attempting to use the value of a weak pointer, you should check whether it is nil.

07---Tech/07---Concepts/Fuzzy-Algorithm.title: Fuzzy Algorithm
07---Tech/07---Concepts/Fuzzy-Algorithm.content:
A fuzzy system is a non-binary concept and computers used to make algorithms for vast software programs, we know this system more by its Searching mechanism.
Based on an article from AddSearch:

Fuzzy logic enables a computer to consider multiple options or degrees of truth when evaluating data rather than the traditional “true or false” (1 or 0) Boolean logic on which current computers are built.

It allows computers to look at problems like humans and is beneficial for developing intelligent systems that solve decision-making, identification, and pattern recognition problems.
Refs:
- What is Fuzzy Search?

07---Tech/07---Concepts/How-USB-Works.title: How USB Works
07---Tech/07---Concepts/How-USB-Works.content:
USB is a serial port.
Other serial ports at not hotplug or auto configable.
All USB variants with their speed:

USB 1.0 [lowSpeed: 1.5Mbs, maxSpeed: 12Mbs]
USB 2.0 [maxSpeed: 480Mbs]
USB 3.0 [maxSpeed: 5Gbs]
USB 3.1 [maxSpeed: 10Gbs]

Because USB is asynchronous, then there is not any clock line
USB is hard in the case of implementations than UART
USB has to kind of connections connectors:

USB A: for host machines like computer (up-stream)
USB B: for client machines like indivisual devices (down-stream)

For sending data a USB uses 2 data lines called (D+, D-), usb uses D+ for Full-speed supported computers and low D- for low-speed supported computers.
USB can send 4 different type of data (4 data transition types for USB):

Control: exchange all the settings and configurations between USB and host
Isochronous: For time critical devices like speakers, that has real-time communication with no error detection
Bulk: devices like printers that accept one big block of data instead of serializing the data (!= Isochronous)
Intertupts: prepherals like mouse and keyboard that needs immidiate attentions, devices that request a service from the PC host

USB Protocol
